questions.explanation: "My name" is a single thing (like it), so it takes is: My name is Sara. Use am only with I, and are with you/we/they.
We always takes are: We are from Tunisia. Am is only for I, and is is only for he/she/it.
The subject I always takes am: I am twelve years old. Note that English uses to be for age (not have): say I am twelve, not "I have twelve".

questions.prompt: Which is the correct way to contract "He is my brother"?
questions.explanation: He is contracts to he's: an apostrophe replaces the i of is. "Hes" is missing the apostrophe, "His" is the possessive adjective (his car), and the 're contraction only works with are.
Books is plural (more than one), so it takes are: The books are on the desk. Use is only for a single thing (the book is on the desk).
"Tom and I" means two people = we, so the verb is are: Tom and I are good friends. A subject joined by and is plural, even when one part is I.
